1: The cities of refuge are established immediately after the allotments are complete — the first communal institution for the new settlement. The command echoes Numbers 35:9-15 and Deuteronomy 19:1-13, which established the theological and legal framework; here Joshua implements what Moses legislated.
2: The cities of refuge (ʿārê hammiqlāṭ) implement a critical distinction in Israelite homicide law: intentional murder versus accidental killing. The background is the blood avenger (gōʾēl haddām, 'redeemer of the blood') — the nearest male relative of a killed person was legally obligated to execute the killer. This kinship-justice system predated Israel and operated throughout the ancient Near East; the asylum city system modifies it by creating a protected space for inadvertent killers while leaving intentional murderers exposed to the avenger.
3: The manslayer who kills 'accidentally or unintentionally' (bišgāgāh ûbĕlōʾ-daʿat) — two terms for inadvertence: šĕgāgāh means mistake/accident (Leviticus 4:2; Numbers 15:24-29 uses it for unintentional sin); bĕlōʾ-daʿat means without knowledge/forethought. The double qualification emphasizes that the asylum is for genuine accidents, not reckless behavior. Numbers 35:16-21 lists categories of intentional murder (using a weapon, lying in wait, acting with enmity) that do NOT qualify for asylum; the distinction between premeditated and accidental death is one of the most sophisticated elements of Mosaic jurisprudence.
4: The asylum procedure at the city gate: the fugitive presents his case to the elders of the city. The city gate was the standard judicial forum in ancient Israel (Ruth 4:1-11; Deuteronomy 21:19; Proverbs 31:23; Amos 5:15); the elders there represented the community's legal authority. The procedure requires a hearing — the manslayer is not given automatic protection but must make a case, allowing the elders to evaluate whether the killing was genuinely accidental. The city then receives him into their community.
5: 'If the blood avenger pursues him, they must not hand the accused over.' The city's protection against the avenger is absolute for an inadvertent killer who follows the procedure. The blood avenger (gōʾēl haddām) operated within a recognized legal right; the asylum city system did not abolish it but overrode it for the specific category of accidental death. The tension between kinship-justice (the avenger's right) and communal discernment (the elders' hearing) was a sophisticated legal balance — the system required both the family's right to seek redress and the community's authority to distinguish murder from accident.
6: 'He is to stay in that city until he has stood trial before the congregation, and until the death of the high priest.' Two release conditions: (1) a formal trial before the assembly (ʿādāh) — the same body that ratified the Gibeonite treaty (Joshua 9:15-21); (2) the death of the incumbent high priest. The high priest's death as a release mechanism is unique in ancient law — there is no ANE parallel. Rabbinic tradition interpreted it as an expiatory death: the high priest's death atones for the blood that was shed, even inadvertently, releasing the manslayer from further liability. The high priest's death-as-expiation is a powerful typological structure that points toward Hebrews' reading of Christ's death as the permanent high-priestly atonement.
7: Three western cities of refuge: Kedesh in Galilee (Naphtali), Shechem in the hill country of Ephraim, and Kiriath-arba/Hebron in the hill country of Judah. The three cities are distributed geographically: north, center, and south. This placement was deliberate and explicitly commanded in Deuteronomy 19:3 ('you shall prepare the roads... so that anyone who kills a person may flee there'). The roads to the cities were supposed to be maintained and clearly marked; Rabbinic tradition (Sifre Deut 186) says the roads were kept wide and the signs well-maintained. Geographic accessibility was a justice concern — a system requiring a long journey to reach asylum was a system that would fail the innocent.
8: Three eastern cities of refuge: Bezer in the plateau of Reuben, Ramoth in Gilead from Gad, and Golan in Bashan from half-Manasseh. The symmetrical distribution (three east, three west) provided access for the entire land, with no region more than a day's travel from an asylum city. Bezer is in the Moabite plateau; Ramoth-Gilead (1 Kings 22:3,29; 2 Kings 8:28; Joshua 9:14) became a major military site in the Syrian wars — the city of refuge eventually became the most contested fortress on Israel's eastern frontier.
9: 'These were the designated cities for all the Israelites and for the foreigners living among them' — the asylum system extended to resident aliens (gēr) as well as native Israelites. The extension to foreigners is explicitly commanded in Numbers 35:15 and reflects the egalitarian principle that the same law applies to native and stranger alike (Numbers 15:15-16; Leviticus 24:22). This is not merely administrative policy but theological: YHWH's justice does not discriminate between citizens and residents. The cities of refuge were a social institution that embodied the covenant's ethic of equal justice — an ethic that the NT presents as fulfilled in Christ, the permanent refuge for all who flee to him.
